text: The global labour market is in the midst of an historic transition, being battered by political and social crisis, rising inequality and a decline in working conditions and employment protection. Meanwhile, a plethora of International Organization policy documents for the ‘safety and well-being of the world of work’ are filling the press and media outlets. Based on 16 semi-direct interviews conducted with stakeholders of the ILO, WHO and World Bank, this piece analyses the ways in which the ILO's Decent Work Agenda ‘sets the agenda’ and takes a critical look into the functioning of global institutional networks of horizontal policy coordination. Our findings are that despite the rhetoric and propaganda on decent work the UN agencies' actions demonstrate clear forms of support and embedding within the UN system as a whole, the inclusion of the concept of Decent Work is fully aligned to the WHO health-as-a-human-right paradigm that resonates well within international labour law; yet conversely the World Bank – reflecting on the conservative Washington consensus, does not formally accept the language and rhetoric contained within the ILO Decent Work of the Agenda – choosing to advance its own vocabulary on the issue of job creation and entrepreneurship in developing countries instead. Moreover, whilst large scale events like the global economic crisis of 2008 create opportunities for campaigns around the advancement of justice at the international level, other barriers remain that challenge the social movement campaigns; in particular issues surrounding not only the adequacy of empirical evidence that demonstrates impact on working conditions and policies at the country level that advances the aims of the Agenda, but also at the more general level in respect of what makes social progress at the global level achievable. This piece sheds light to the intricate interactions among international institutions at a time when global debates around future of work and inequalities are a focus of politics, research and action world-wide.
explanation: Text displays domain-specific critique with integrated references to ILO/WHO/World Bank and a nuanced, argument-driven structure typical of scholarly writing. AI-generated prose often lacks such precise policy context and depth, making human authorship more probable.
